instruction: How do I set up a Rust workspace with multiple crates, shared dependencies, and feature gates?
output:
<think>
Rust workspaces are defined in a root Cargo.toml with [workspace] and members list. All crates in the workspace share a single Cargo.lock (consistent dependency versions) and a single target/ directory (shared compilation cache).

The key design decisions: (1) binary crate vs library crate - lib crates are reusable, bin crates are executables. Most projects have one lib and one or more bins. (2) Feature gates let you conditionally compile code, which is essential for optional dependencies and reducing compile time. (3) workspace.dependencies (Cargo 1.64+) lets you define dependency versions once in the root and inherit them in member crates.

For feature gates: define features in Cargo.toml, use #[cfg(feature = "x")] in code. Features should be additive (enabling a feature should never break existing functionality). Common pattern: a "full" feature that enables everything, individual features for optional backends.

The workspace.dependencies feature is a game-changer for large projects - you specify the version once in the root Cargo.toml, then each member crate says `dep = { workspace = true }` to inherit it.
</think>

```rust
// === Root Cargo.toml ===
// [workspace]
// members = ["crates/*"]
// resolver = "2"  # required for edition 2021+
//
// [workspace.dependencies]
// serde = { version = "1.0", features = ["derive"] }
// tokio = { version = "1", features = ["full"] }
// anyhow = "1.0"
// tracing = "0.1"
//
// # Workspace layout:
// # project/
// # +-- Cargo.toml (workspace root)
// # +-- Cargo.lock (shared)
// # +-- crates/
// #     +-- core/        (library)
// #     +-- api/         (binary)
// #     +-- worker/      (binary)
// #     +-- shared/      (library, internal)

// === crates/core/Cargo.toml ===
// [package]
// name = "myapp-core"
// version = "0.1.0"
// edition = "2021"
//
// [features]
// default = ["postgres"]
// postgres = ["dep:sqlx"]
// sqlite = ["dep:sqlx"]
// full = ["postgres", "sqlite", "redis"]
// redis = ["dep:redis"]
//
// [dependencies]
// serde = { workspace = true }
// anyhow = { workspace = true }
// sqlx = { version = "0.7", optional = true, features = ["runtime-tokio"] }
// redis = { version = "0.23", optional = true }

// === crates/core/src/lib.rs ===
use serde::{Serialize, Deserialize};

#[derive(Debug, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub struct User {
    pub id: i64,
    pub name: String,
    pub email: String,
}

// Feature-gated database backends
#[cfg(feature = "postgres")]
pub mod postgres {
    use super::User;
    pub async fn get_user(id: i64) -> anyhow::Result<User> {
        // PostgreSQL implementation
        Ok(User { id, name: "Alice".into(), email: "alice@example.com".into() })
    }
}

#[cfg(feature = "sqlite")]
pub mod sqlite {
    use super::User;
    pub async fn get_user(id: i64) -> anyhow::Result<User> {
        Ok(User { id, name: "Alice".into(), email: "alice@example.com".into() })
    }
}

// === crates/api/Cargo.toml ===
// [package]
// name = "myapp-api"
// version = "0.1.0"
// edition = "2021"
//
// [dependencies]
// myapp-core = { path = "../core", features = ["postgres"] }
// tokio = { workspace = true }
// tracing = { workspace = true }

// Build commands:
// cargo build                          # build all workspace members
// cargo build -p myapp-api             # build specific crate
// cargo test --workspace               # test everything
// cargo test -p myapp-core             # test specific crate
// cargo run -p myapp-api               # run specific binary
// cargo build -p myapp-core --features full  # enable all features
```

instruction: When should I use a binary crate vs a library crate in Rust, and how do I structure a project with both?
output:
<think>
Library crates (lib.rs) produce reusable code that other crates can depend on. Binary crates (main.rs) produce executables. Most real projects need both: the library contains the core logic, and the binary is a thin wrapper that handles CLI args, config, and calls the library.

This separation is important because: (1) the library can be tested without spinning up the full binary, (2) other binaries (CLI tool, server, worker) can share the same library, (3) integration tests can import the library directly, and (4) the library can be published to crates.io independently.

The standard pattern: src/lib.rs for core logic, src/main.rs for the CLI entry point that calls lib functions. For multiple binaries, use the src/bin/ directory - each .rs file becomes a separate binary.

The examples/ directory is for demonstration code that shows how to use the library. `cargo run --example foo` runs them. They are compiled but not included in the published crate.
</think>

```rust
// Project layout:
// myapp/
// +-- Cargo.toml
// +-- src/
// |   +-- lib.rs        (library: core logic)
// |   +-- main.rs       (default binary: CLI)
// |   +-- bin/
// |       +-- server.rs  (additional binary)
// |       +-- worker.rs  (additional binary)
// +-- examples/
// |   +-- basic_usage.rs
// +-- tests/
//     +-- integration_test.rs

// === src/lib.rs (the library) ===
pub mod config;
pub mod processor;

pub struct App {
    config: config::AppConfig,
}

impl App {
    pub fn new(config: config::AppConfig) -> Self {
        App { config }
    }

    pub fn run(&self) -> anyhow::Result<String> {
        let result = processor::process(&self.config.input)?;
        Ok(format!("Processed: {}", result))
    }
}

// === src/config.rs ===
pub mod config {
    #[derive(Debug)]
    pub struct AppConfig {
        pub input: String,
        pub verbose: bool,
    }
}

pub mod processor {
    pub fn process(input: &str) -> anyhow::Result<String> {
        Ok(input.to_uppercase())
    }
}

// === src/main.rs (thin CLI wrapper) ===
use myapp::{App, config::AppConfig};

fn main() -> anyhow::Result<()> {
    let config = AppConfig {
        input: std::env::args().nth(1).unwrap_or_default(),
        verbose: std::env::args().any(|a| a == "--verbose"),
    };
    let app = App::new(config);
    println!("{}", app.run()?);
    Ok(())
}

// === src/bin/server.rs (separate binary, same library) ===
// use myapp::App;
// fn main() { /* HTTP server using App */ }

// === examples/basic_usage.rs ===
// use myapp::processor;
// fn main() {
//     let result = processor::process("hello").unwrap();
//     println!("{}", result);
// }

// === tests/integration_test.rs ===
// use myapp::App;
// #[test]
// fn test_full_pipeline() {
//     let app = App::new(...);
//     assert!(app.run().is_ok());
// }

// Commands:
// cargo run              # runs src/main.rs
// cargo run --bin server # runs src/bin/server.rs
// cargo run --example basic_usage
// cargo test             # runs unit + integration tests
```

instruction: How do I use Rust feature gates effectively to manage optional dependencies and conditional compilation?
output:
<think>
Feature gates are Rust's mechanism for conditional compilation. They control which code is compiled and which optional dependencies are included. The rules:

1. Features must be ADDITIVE. Enabling a feature should never break existing code. This is the fundamental design constraint. If feature A and feature B can't be enabled simultaneously, your design is wrong.

2. Use `dep:` syntax (Rust 1.60+) for optional dependencies. Before this, adding `foo = { optional = true }` automatically created a feature named "foo", which was confusing. Now `dep:foo` in the features table is explicit.

3. Default features are what users get without specifying anything. Put commonly-needed features in default. Users can opt out with `default-features = false`.

4. Feature unification means if any crate in the dependency graph enables a feature, it is enabled for all. This is why features must be additive and why you should not use features for mutually exclusive configs.

Common patterns: a "full" meta-feature enabling everything, backend selection (postgres/sqlite/mysql), serialization format selection (json/bincode/msgpack).
</think>

```rust
// === Cargo.toml ===
// [package]
// name = "mylib"
// version = "0.1.0"
//
// [features]
// default = ["json", "logging"]  # what users get by default
// full = ["json", "msgpack", "logging", "metrics", "postgres", "redis"]
//
// # Serialization backends
// json = ["dep:serde_json"]
// msgpack = ["dep:rmp-serde"]
//
// # Observability
// logging = ["dep:tracing"]
// metrics = ["dep:prometheus"]
//
// # Storage backends
// postgres = ["dep:sqlx"]
// redis = ["dep:redis"]
//
// [dependencies]
// serde = { version = "1", features = ["derive"] }  # always included
// serde_json = { version = "1", optional = true }
// rmp-serde = { version = "1", optional = true }
// tracing = { version = "0.1", optional = true }
// prometheus = { version = "0.13", optional = true }
// sqlx = { version = "0.7", optional = true }
// redis = { version = "0.23", optional = true }

// === src/lib.rs ===
use serde::{Serialize, Deserialize};

#[derive(Debug, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub struct Event {
    pub id: String,
    pub payload: String,
}

// Feature-gated serialization
#[cfg(feature = "json")]
pub fn to_json(event: &Event) -> Result<String, serde_json::Error> {
    serde_json::to_string(event)
}

#[cfg(feature = "msgpack")]
pub fn to_msgpack(event: &Event) -> Result<Vec<u8>, rmp_serde::encode::Error> {
    rmp_serde::to_vec(event)
}

// Feature-gated logging
pub fn process(event: &Event) {
    #[cfg(feature = "logging")]
    tracing::info!(event_id = %event.id, "Processing event");

    // Core logic always compiles
    println!("Processing: {}", event.id);

    #[cfg(feature = "metrics")]
    {
        // prometheus::counter!("events_processed", 1);
    }
}

// Compile-time feature validation
#[cfg(all(not(feature = "json"), not(feature = "msgpack")))]
compile_error!("At least one serialization backend must be enabled (json or msgpack)");

// Users:
// mylib = "0.1"                              # gets default (json + logging)
// mylib = { version = "0.1", features = ["full"] }  # everything
// mylib = { version = "0.1", default-features = false, features = ["msgpack"] }
```

instruction: How do I structure a Rust project with examples, benchmarks, and integration tests properly?
output:
<think>
Rust's project layout conventions are well-defined by Cargo:

- src/lib.rs and src/main.rs: the crate root
- tests/: integration tests (each file is a separate test binary, compiled against the public API)
- examples/: example programs showing library usage
- benches/: benchmarks (using criterion or the nightly bench harness)

Integration tests in tests/ only see the public API (like an external user). Unit tests in src/ can test private functions with #[cfg(test)] mod tests. This distinction is important: integration tests prove your public API works; unit tests prove internal logic is correct.

For examples, each file in examples/ becomes a separate binary. Use `cargo run --example name` to run them. Examples serve as documentation and are tested by CI (they must compile, even if not explicitly tested).

Benchmarks with criterion go in benches/ and need a [[bench]] entry in Cargo.toml with `harness = false` to use criterion's custom harness instead of the built-in one.

For larger projects, use `tests/common/mod.rs` for shared test utilities (this special path avoids Cargo treating it as a separate test binary).
</think>

```rust
// Project layout:
// mylib/
// +-- Cargo.toml
// +-- src/
// |   +-- lib.rs          (library root)
// |   +-- parser.rs       (module with unit tests)
// +-- tests/
// |   +-- common/
// |   |   +-- mod.rs      (shared test utilities, NOT a test binary)
// |   +-- parsing_test.rs (integration test)
// |   +-- api_test.rs     (integration test)
// +-- examples/
// |   +-- basic.rs
// |   +-- advanced.rs
// +-- benches/
//     +-- parser_bench.rs

// === src/parser.rs (with unit tests) ===
pub fn parse(input: &str) -> Vec<&str> {
    input.split_whitespace().collect()
}

// Private helper — only testable via unit tests
fn normalize(s: &str) -> String {
    s.trim().to_lowercase()
}

#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;

    #[test]
    fn test_parse_basic() {
        assert_eq!(parse("hello world"), vec!["hello", "world"]);
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_normalize() {
        // Can test private functions
        assert_eq!(normalize("  Hello  "), "hello");
    }
}

// === tests/common/mod.rs (shared test utilities) ===
// pub fn setup_test_data() -> Vec<String> {
//     vec!["test input one".into(), "test input two".into()]
// }

// === tests/parsing_test.rs (integration test) ===
// mod common;  // imports tests/common/mod.rs
// use mylib::parse;
//
// #[test]
// fn test_parse_integration() {
//     let inputs = common::setup_test_data();
//     for input in &inputs {
//         let result = parse(input);
//         assert!(!result.is_empty());
//     }
// }

// === examples/basic.rs ===
// use mylib::parse;
// fn main() {
//     let words = parse("hello world from rust");
//     println!("Parsed {} words: {:?}", words.len(), words);
// }

// === benches/parser_bench.rs ===
// Cargo.toml needs: [[bench]] name = "parser_bench" harness = false
// use criterion::{criterion_group, criterion_main, Criterion};
// use mylib::parse;
//
// fn bench_parse(c: &mut Criterion) {
//     let input = "word ".repeat(10000);
//     c.bench_function("parse_10k_words", |b| {
//         b.iter(|| parse(criterion::black_box(&input)));
//     });
// }
// criterion_group!(benches, bench_parse);
// criterion_main!(benches);

// Commands:
// cargo test                    # unit + integration tests
// cargo test --lib              # unit tests only
// cargo test --test parsing_test # specific integration test
// cargo run --example basic     # run example
// cargo bench                   # run benchmarks
```
